assignment.name: multi_agent
assignment.title: Design and build a multi-agent system for a real task
assignment.description: Design, implement, and evaluate a multi-agent system that solves a concrete task. The system should exhibit meaningful coordination among agents (planning, branching, delegation, or aggregation) and ship with a runnable artifact, evaluation evidence, and a written report.
constraints: Must include at least two distinct agent roles with different responsibilities.
Must produce traceable logs showing inter-agent communication.
Must include >=5 evaluation cases and >=2 documented failure cases.
Total cost per representative run should remain reasonable (token-bounded).
Must be reproducible from the repo with a single documented command.

recommended_plan: Planning & scheduling (e.g., meeting scheduler or project task coordinator with planner and executor agents)
Simple domain (meeting scheduler: parse requests, check calendars, propose times, handle confirmations)

verdict.notes: Meeting scheduler is a solid, scoped choice with clear agent roles (planner/executor) and natural coordination opportunities. However, calendar state management and confirmation logic can become complex fast—front-load a minimal mock calendar backend and define failure modes (conflicts, unavailability) upfront to avoid last-minute scope creep. The 14-day deadline is tight but achievable if you fix the domain boundaries early and use a lightweight framework (e.g., Claude API calls with structured logging).

root.chosen_best_option: Planning & scheduling (e.g., meeting scheduler or project task coordinator with planner and executor agents)

branches.option: Document analysis & summarization (e.g., multi-agent literature review or contract analysis with specialized agents)

history: Document analysis & summarization (e.g., multi-agent literature review or contract analysis with specialized agents)

verdict.notes: Document analysis is a solid, well-scoped choice with clear agent roles (coordinator + specialists), but the plan lacks specificity on evaluation methodology, failure injection strategy, and cost management. Recommend immediately defining: (1) exact document domain & size, (2) evaluation metrics & test case generation, (3) token budget per run and fallback strategies, (4) which 2 failure modes to deliberately trigger—this will consume 2-3 days of your 14-day window if deferred.

child.chosen_best_option: Hierarchical (e.g., Coordinator + Specialist agents with explicit delegation flow)

history: Document analysis & summarization (e.g., multi-agent literature review or contract analysis with specialized agents)
Peer-based (e.g., Reader + Analyzer + Synthesizer agents with message passing)

verdict.notes: The document analysis + peer-based architecture is feasible and directly addresses coordination requirements, but the plan lacks critical implementation details: no specification of the concrete task, agent communication protocol, or evaluation methodology. You need to immediately nail down the specific domain (contracts, research papers, etc.), define message schemas, and design your 5+ test cases before coding to avoid scope creep in the final week.

branches.option: Medium domain (project task coordinator: decompose goals into subtasks, assign to agents, track dependencies, handle blockers)

history: Planning & scheduling (e.g., meeting scheduler or project task coordinator with planner and executor agents)
Medium domain (project task coordinator: decompose goals into subtasks, assign to agents, track dependencies, handle blockers)

verdict.notes: The project task coordinator is a solid, well-scoped domain that naturally fits multi-agent patterns (planner/executor with clear communication). Primary risks: (1) coordination logic complexity may balloon if dependency tracking and blocker resolution aren't carefully bounded—define a maximum of 3-4 task levels and 2-3 blocker types upfront; (2) evaluation evidence (5+ cases + 2 failures) is tight on a 14-day deadline; start with synthetic test cases rather than real data to hit reproducibility and cost constraints quickly. Recommend building a minimal viable task graph (3-5 tasks max), automated test harness, and pre-written eval cases by day 5 to leave buffer for polish and report.
